captured.evidence: enters the central compartment first — the blood and vessel-rich group including brain, heart, kidneys, and liver. This group receives about 75% of cardiac output despite being only 10% of body mass

wakes up in 8 minutes NOT because of metabolism — hepatic metabolism is too slow. It is redistribution: propofol moves from the highly perfused brain into less-perfused peripheral tissues like muscle and fat, dropping brain concentration below the threshold for consciousness
context-sensitive half-time — the time for plasma concentration to fall 50% after stopping an infusion of a given duration
after 4 hours the peripheral compartments have progressively saturated with propofol. The redistribution gradient is gone. Recovery depends on slower hepatic metabolism
First-pass pulmonary uptake buffers peak arterial concentrations of lipophilic drugs — fentanyl undergoes 65-75% first-pass pulmonary uptake protecting the brain
elderly patients have smaller effective central compartments, producing higher peak concentrations from the same dose — dose reduction is essential

q3-A-excellent.voss_quip: Finally, someone who understands redistribution isn't just a fancy word.

captured.evidence: brain is in the vessel-rich group which receives a large portion of cardiac output
wakes up quickly because the drug redistributes from the brain to muscle and fat, not because of hepatic metabolism
Context-sensitive half-time describes how the time for plasma levels to drop 50% changes depending on infusion duration

After a long infusion, the muscle and fat compartments are already saturated with drug, so there is no concentration gradient for redistribution anymore. Recovery then depends on the liver metabolizing the drug

captured.evidence: Phase I reactions are functionalization reactions — oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis — that expose or introduce functional groups on the drug molecule. These prepare it for Phase II but can also produce active or toxic metabolites.

Phase II reactions are conjugation reactions, most commonly glucuronidation by UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, attaching polar groups to make highly water-soluble, usually inactive metabolites for renal or biliary excretion.

CYP3A4 is the most abundant hepatic CYP isoenzyme, responsible for roughly 50% of all drug metabolism. It handles fentanyl, sufentanil, alfentanil, and midazolam. Ketoconazole and grapefruit juice inhibit CYP3A4; rifampin induces it.

Remifentanil contains a methyl ester linkage hydrolyzed by nonspecific tissue and plasma esterases, NOT pseudocholinesterase and NOT CYP450. This organ-independent metabolism gives a CSHT of 3-5 minutes regardless of infusion duration or hepatic/renal function.
Halothane hepatitis results from reductive anaerobic Phase I metabolism producing reactive trifluoroacetyl chloride intermediates triggering immune-mediated hepatocyte destruction.
Codeine is a prodrug requiring CYP2D6 O-demethylation to morphine — poor metabolizers get no analgesia, ultrarapid metabolizers risk dangerous morphine levels.

q6-A-excellent.summary: Perfect recall of all key pharmacokinetic concepts with accurate details on drug metabolism pathways. Your understanding of remifentanil's unique esterase-based metabolism is spot-on.
q6-A-excellent.voss_quip: Finally, someone who knows esterases aren't pseudocholinesterase. Textbook perfect.

captured.evidence: Phase I involves oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis that modify the drug and can produce active or toxic metabolites

CYP3A4 is the major CYP enzyme handling about half of drug metabolism including fentanyl and midazolam
tissue esterases hydrolyze it through ester hydrolysis, not CYP enzymes. Organ-independent metabolism keeps CSHT at 3-5 minutes no matter how long the infusion runs

captured.evidence: Lidocaine is a high hepatic extraction ratio drug, extraction ratio above 0.7, same category as propofol and morphine. For high extraction drugs, clearance is flow-limited — determined primarily by hepatic blood flow, not enzyme activity or protein binding. In cardiogenic shock, reduced cardiac output directly reduces hepatic blood flow and therefore lidocaine clearance.

renal clearance involves three mechanisms: glomerular filtration of unbound drug passively, active tubular secretion into the lumen, and passive tubular reabsorption back into blood. Net excretion equals filtration plus secretion minus reabsorption.

The creatinine of 1.0 is misleading because this frail 82-year-old has very little muscle mass. Less muscle means less creatinine production, masking severely reduced GFR. Cockcroft-Gault or CKD-EPI equations give accurate estimates for drug dosing.

alkalinizing urine with sodium bicarbonate ionizes weakly acidic drugs like phenobarbital and salicylates in the tubular lumen, trapping them and preventing reabsorption. Fetal ion trapping — fetal blood is more acidic than maternal, trapping weak base local anesthetics in fetal circulation.

Glutathione conjugation detoxifies NAPQI, the toxic metabolite of acetaminophen. In overdose, glutathione depletion allows NAPQI accumulation causing centrilobular hepatic necrosis. N-acetylcysteine replenishes glutathione.

q9b-A-excellent.summary: Comprehensive answer covering all key pharmacokinetic principles with accurate clinical correlations. Correctly explained flow-limited clearance, renal mechanisms, creatinine limitations in elderly, ion trapping, and acetaminophen toxicity.
q9b-A-excellent.voss_quip: Finally, someone who actually understands pharmacokinetics instead of memorizing flashcards.

captured.evidence: Lidocaine has a high extraction ratio meaning its clearance depends on hepatic blood flow. In cardiogenic shock, blood flow to the liver is reduced so the drug accumulates regardless of enzyme function.
For renal clearance, drugs are filtered at the glomerulus, some are actively secreted, and some are reabsorbed.
The creatinine is misleading because she has low muscle mass producing less creatinine. A normal level does not mean normal kidney function in the elderly. You need Cockcroft-Gault equations.
